intro:
A garrison was a detachment of soldiers stationed in a fortified position to hold it against enemy attack. In the period of the united monarchy, Philistine garrisons were stationed within Israelite territory as an instrument of political and military control. Jonathan's attack on the Philistine garrison at Geba sparked the beginning of Saul's war against Philistia (1 Samuel 13:3–4), and the garrison at Michmash Pass was an important military objective in the subsequent campaign (1 Samuel 13:23; 14:1). David later placed garrisons in Edom and Syria-Damascus after his conquests (2 Samuel 8:6, 14).

Jonathan's famous lone attack with his armor-bearer on the Philistine garrison at Michmash (1 Samuel 14:12–14) resulted in a rout that the LORD amplified into a general panic and a major Israelite victory. Garrison duty represented the frontline military presence of an occupying power, and striking or defeating a garrison carried both practical and symbolic significance — it demonstrated that the occupier's grip could be broken. The term also appears in Paul's description of Damascus, where the governor under King Aretas kept the city with a garrison to seize him (2 Corinthians 11:32).
